title: A Simple Approach to Adversarial Robustness in Few-shot Image Classification

paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## A SIMPLE APPROACH TO ADVERSARIAL ROBUSTNESS - IN FEW SHOT IMAGE CLASSIFICATION

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Few-shot image classification, where the goal is to generalize to tasks with limited labeled data, has seen great progress over the years. However, the classifiers are vulnerable to adversarial examples, posing a question regarding their generalization capabilities. Recent works have tried to combine meta-learning approaches with adversarial training to improve the robustness of few-shot classifiers. We show that a simple transfer-learning based approach can be used to train adversarially robust few-shot classifiers. We also present a method for novel classification task based on calibrating the centroid of the few-shot category towards the base classes. We show that standard adversarial training on base categories along with centroid-based classifier in the novel categories, outperforms or is on-par with state-of-the-art advanced methods on standard benchmarks such as Mini-ImageNet, CIFAR-FS and CUB datasets. Our method is simple and easy to scale, and with little effort

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Few-shot learning presents the challenge of generalizing to unseen tasks with limited data. The problem is aimed at learning quickly from few examples of data, which is generally considered the hallmark of human intelligence. This is an important practical problem due to the scarce availability of fully annotated data in the real world. Researchers have shown that such a setting can be considered for various real world computer vision tasks such as image classification (Finn et al., 2017; Chen et al., 2019), object detection (Wang et al., 2020), image segmentation (Rakelly et al., 2018), facerecognition (Guo et al., 2020) and medical analysis (Maicas et al., 2018). As a result, it is of paramount importance that such safety-critical systems are reliable and robust to changes in input. Specifically in this work, we consider robustness to adversarial examples - carefully crafted perturbations using gradients that when added to inputs, _fool_ the classifier. The most common method of improving robustness is by adversarial training (Goodfellow et al., 2015) which involves training on adversarial examples using adversary of choice. Traditional adversarially robust methods (Madry et al., 2018; Goodfellow et al., 2015; Szegedy et al., 2013) consider a data-rich setting where many examples are available per category. It has also been shown that adversarial generalization possibly requires significantly more data (Schmidt et al., 2018). This becomes challenging in a scenario where the end-user has access to limited amount of annotated data but is interested in building a robust few-shot classifier. Such a setting is more practical and it is important to develop methods which can work with minimal effort in the pre-deployment stage. We show from our experiments that a simple approach of finetuning the network on clean data from an adversarially robust base model can lead to significant improvement in robustness with minimal resources.

Previous works on improving robustness for few-shot classifiers focus mainly on meta-learning approaches. Here the base model is trained on adversarial examples of episodic data. We show that standard adversarial training on a large dataset is sufficient for learning a robust classifier. This makes our method simple and scalable, making the process of training robust classifiers straightforward and also creating directions to explore methods from robustness literature for few-shot setting.

It is important to understand the problem of few-shot learning in order to develop their robust counterparts. The goal in few-shot learning is to learn transferable knowledge for generalization to tasks with limited data. These have generally been partitioned into metric learning (Snell et al., 2017; Vinyals et al., 2016; Sung et al., 2018), optimization-based (Finn et al., 2017; Ravi & Larochelle,

2016) and hallucination based methods (Hariharan & Girshick, 2017; Yang et al., 2021; Antoniou et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2018). The most common work among optimization based methods is MAML (Finn et al., 2017) which aims at learning a network initialization using a bi-level optimization procedure, that when finetuned on limited data is able to generalize to the new task. Recent works have shown that meta-learning methods can be extended to include adversarial robustness as well. (Goldblum et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2021) perform adversarial training on top of meta-learners to improve robustness significantly. However, adversarial training on its own is expensive and combining with meta-learning makes the problem computationally intensive. (Wang et al., 2021) showed that there exists a compromise between training robust meta-learners and performance, both in terms of standard and robust accuracy, motivating the need for a simpler approach.

Another interesting line of work, focused on improving few-shot learning, shows that a simple method which involves training on large scale data and finetuning the model on the few-shot dataset can match or even outperform meta-learning methods (Chen et al., 2019; Dhillon et al., 2019). The intuition is that the model sees examples from all categories and can get a general sense of semantics rather than seeing only episodic data. Such a setting also makes it easier to train on large scale data that can lead to further improvements as shown in (Dhillon et al., 2019). Our results show considering a simple setting can be beneficial for adversarial robustness as well.

We consider the few-shot setting and show that adversarial training along with simple nearest centroid based classifier can outperform previous methods in terms of robustness. Such a setting is practically relevant, since the adversarial training on large data needs to be done just once and robustness for few-shot classes can be achieved without creating adversarial examples for the specific task. We believe it also becomes easier to incorporate new approaches to robustness, such as verifiably robust classifiers (Gowal et al., 2018; Cohen et al., 2019) and can bring together robust methods for both large and limited dataset settings.

In the following sections, we describe our method and discuss relevant related work. We present experimental findings and implementation details in the subsequent sections followed by conclusion and directions for future work.

## 2 METHOD

Here we introduce notation and provide a description of our method. Our first objective is to learn a feature extractor _fθb_ and linear classifier _Cωb_ using the abundantly-labeled base dataset _Xb_ . At the next stage, when a _N_ -way _K_ -shot few-shot task is sampled from the novel dataset _Xn_ , we use only the feature extractor _fθb_ and learn a new linear classifier _Cωn_ such that it can generalize to unseen examples from the novel categories. We show that a simple approach of training a robust base model and then adapting it to novel categories can outperform previous approaches. We divide our approach into two stages: (1) Robust Base training and (2) Novel training.

## 2.1 ROBUST BASE TRAINING

Given a base dataset _Xb_ with large number of annotated examples per category, we perform adversarial training using an iterative adversary such as PGD (Madry et al., 2018). Specifically, we solve the min-max objective

Here, _L_ ( _θ, x, y_ ) represents the training objective, which is commonly cross-entropy and _θ_ = ( _θb, ωb_ ) represents the combination of the feature extractor and base classifier parameters. There are different methods for optimizing the inner maximization in Equation 1. For all our experiments we use the Projected Gradient Descent (PGD) algorithm, an iterative algorithm presented in (Madry et al., 2018) with _p_ = _∞_ which corresponds to finding a perturbation _δ_ around an _ϵ_ -bounded hypercube around _x_ that maximizes the objective. Once we find the perturbation, the perturbed input is added to the training set and parameters are tuned. This method is called adversarial training and is the most widely used method to improve robustness to adversarial examples. Note that adversarial training, which is a computationally expensive procedure, needs to be performed just once using base dataset.

In the next stage, we use only clean examples which makes it practical and easy to train robust

**Weight averaging:** Weight averaging (WA) has been shown as a simple way to improve generalization (Izmailov et al., 2018; Garipov et al., 2018) in deep networks as it approximates ensembling in temporal fashion and can find flatter optima in loss surface. This method has been used in adversarial training (Gowal et al., 2020; Chen et al.) for improving robustness in standard classification task. Since we are interested in using base parameters at the next stage, we perform weight averaging for only the feature extractor parameters _θb_ and show this can be used for few-shot setting.

Similar to (Gowal et al., 2020), we keep a separate copy of the weights and for every iteration perform exponential moving average method _θb[′] ← τθb[′]_ + (1 _− τ_ ) _∗ θb_ and use _θb[′]_ during the evaluation. We set _τ_ = 0 _._ 999 in all our experiments.

## 2.2 NOVEL TRAINING

During this stage, we consider the _N_ -way, _K_ -shot method as the novel task and adapt our learnt feature extractor _fθb_ using classifier _Cωn_ . For all our experiments, we found best results when the weights of feature extractor _fθb_ are frozen and not optimized during novel training. Intuitively, this can be understood not wanting the parameters of the feature extractor to be biased towards the fewshot examples. And since we are interested in learning only from clean data during the novel training, there can also be an effect of _forgetting_ the robustness learnt at the base stage. This was observed in (Goldblum et al., 2019) where only the final layer was trained and rest of the parameters were frozen. During the novel training stage, we use only clean examples and not adversarial examples, making the process straightforward.

**Linear classifier:** The simplest possible baseline is to learn a linear model on top of the frozen feature extractor using the few shot examples of novel categories. As shown in our experiments, this simple baseline on its own achieves reasonable performance compared to previous approaches. This baseline also suggests that a robust base classifier corresponds to a robust novel classifier and a simple approach such as ours is enough to achieve robustness for few-shot classifiers. However, as observed in previous works (Wang et al., 2021; Goldblum et al., 2019) and in our experiments, this approach alone is not sufficient to achieve improved robustness. Interestingly, we achieve much closer results to state of the art compared to previous works using this simple baseline. One challenge associated with using few-shot data is that the model can become biased towards the specific samples and may not capture the true class distribution. Hence there is a need for more calibrated classifiers.

**Background on Distribution Calibation (DC) (Yang et al., 2021):** Recent work (Yang et al., 2021) has shown that standard accuracy of few-shot classifiers can be improved by using Distribution Calibration. They present a _free-lunch_ hallucination-based method where the feature distributions of the novel categories are calibrated using the base dataset, due to the similarity between the base and novel datasets. The mean and covariance of each novel category is calibrated using the statistic of base data. They use these statistics to _hallucinate_ or sample many points from a Gaussian distribution, and learn a logistic regression classifier. This simple method was shown to improve standard accuracy significantly under various settings.

## **Mean Calibration and Nearest Centroid (NC):**

DC method can be computationally expensive due to the calculation of covariance matrix which can be of _O_ ( _N ∗ D_[2] ) complexity where _D_ is the dimensionality of the feature space and _N_ is the number of data points in the base dataset. The covariance matrix is also expensive to store in memory. Moreover, sampling from a multivariate Gaussian with non-diagonal covariance is also expensive and can be of the order of at least _O_ ( _D_[2] _[.]_[3] ) (Bishop, 2006). These can reduce the applicability of the approach for certain architectures.

Another aspect of using the hallucinated features is that the model can become biased to the clean features and learn a non-robust final classifier. Note that since these additional data points are generated in the feature space and not the image space, it is not possible to create adversarial versions of these features and perform adversarial training using the large set of features. This poses a problem of improving the performance during novel training without sacrificing robustness.

To overcome these problems, we present a simple method where we rely only on the calibrated mean and classify query sample using a non-parametric Nearest Centroid based algorithm. We find the nearest base-category centers to each novel training sample and then average them along with the novel training sample to obtain the new mean or centroid for the novel category, similar to (Yang et al., 2021). We do not consider the covariance matrix in our method and we find this approximation works equally well in our experiments. More formally:

At the inference time, the Nearest Centroid (NC) method requires less memory and computation compared to a Nearest Neighbor classifier since we only have to store and compare with one prototype per class rather than the entire training set.

## 3 RELATED WORK

**Few shot Image Classification:** Few-shot learning is a challenging problem in computer vision where the goal is rapid generalization to unseen tasks. Metric learning approaches such as (Snell et al., 2017; Vinyals et al., 2016; Sung et al., 2018) were some of the earliest approaches towards tacking this problem. (Snell et al., 2017) learn a metric space where prototypical representation of each category is utilized for classifying novel data. The prototypes are calculated to be the euclidean average over the support examples. (Ravichandran et al., 2019) showed that rather than taking the average, learning the prototypes along with the model can lead to better performance. More recently, a family of algorithms based on learning to learn (Andrychowicz et al., 2016) or meta-learning have gained considerable attention. (Ravi & Larochelle, 2016) develop an LSTM based meta-learner which learns the optimization algorithm required to train another neural network. (Finn et al., 2017; Nichol et al., 2018) create a model agnostic algorithm which aims to learn a good initialization that can be finetuned easily within a few gradient steps to adapt to new tasks. Hallucination based methods such as (Hariharan & Girshick, 2017; Wang et al., 2018; Antoniou et al., 2017) also present promising directions towards improved generalization. (Gidaris & Komodakis, 2018; Qi et al., 2018) try to directly predict the weights of the classifier for novel categories. (Yang et al., 2021) calibrate the distributions of few shot examples using the statistics of categories with larger number of examples. . However a recent line of work have questioned the need for metalearning or metric learning approaches and showed that simple baselines which are non-episodic in nature can provide competitive performance for few-shot image classification task (Chen et al., 2019; Dhillon et al., 2019). Such line of work provide for non-sophisticated baselines and pose a question to the community to rethink the approach towards few shot learning.

**Adversarial examples:** Adversarial examples are carefully crafted perturbations designed to fool the model (Szegedy et al., 2013; Goodfellow et al., 2015; Kurakin et al., 2016). (Goodfellow et al., 2015) showed that adversarial examples can be created rather easily using the sign of single gradient step, which they called as Fast Gradient Sign Method (FGSM). The existence of such examples raises a

question regarding the generalization capabilities of Deep Neural networks while also posing a threat to practical deployment. Many defenses have been proposed to overcome this problem (Papernot et al., 2016; Xie et al., 2017; Feinman et al., 2017; Li & Li, 2016), but they have been bypassed with slight modifications to the adversary (Athalye et al., 2018; Carlini & Wagner; 2017). One of the most common approaches called adversarial training involves incorporating the adversarial examples into the training set. (Madry et al., 2018) showed that the first order adversary, based on the Projected Gradient Descent (PGD) algorithm can be used to train robust neural networks. Provable methods have developed which can provide certification on the susceptibility of an input towards adversaries (Wong & Kolter, 2018; Wong et al., 2018; Raghunathan et al., 2018; Gowal et al., 2018). A class of algorithms based on randomized smoothing (Cohen et al., 2019; Yang et al., 2020) have shown promising results in training large scale neural networks. (Zhang et al., 2019a) provide a theoretical analysis on the robustness vs accuracy trade-off, which had been studied empirically (Tsipras et al., 2018), and show that their algorithm named TRADES improves robustness compared to previous approaches. (Shafahi et al., 2019) use the gradients from the backpropagation algorithm to improve robustness with minimal cost.

**Adversarial Robustness for Few-shot classifiers:** Recent works have tried to address the problem of adversarial examples in the context of few-shot learning. (Yin et al., 2018) was one of the first works to discuss this problem. They used the FGSM adversary to create adversarial examples and optimized a meta-learner to be robust to adversarial examples. (Goldblum et al., 2019) showed that meta-learning algorithms can be supplemented with adversarial examples in the query set to learn robustness. Their method called Adversarial Querying was shown to be robust to strong attacks such as PGD. Many meta-learning approaches were extended to their robust counterparts by including adversarial query examples. Recently, (Wang et al., 2021) also proposed a similar approach where MAML was used as the base meta learning algorithm. (Wang et al., 2021) also showed that including a contrastive learning objective similar to (Chen et al., 2020) can provide a way to use unlabelled data when learning the model and thus improve both standard and robust accuracy.

## 4 EXPERIMENTS

In this section, we describe our experiments and provide implementation details. We consider three benchmark datasets - **Mini-ImageNet** , **CIFAR-FS** and **CUB** for our experiments. **Mini-ImageNet** consists of 100 classes derived from ImageNet dataset (Russakovsky et al., 2015) where each category consists of 600 images. This was first proposed in (Vinyals et al., 2016) and recent works follow the split provided by (Ravi & Larochelle, 2016) consisting of 64 base, 16 validation and 20 novel classes. We use 84x84x3 images for all experiments with Mini-ImageNet. **CIFAR-FS** was proposed in (Bertinetto et al., 2016) as a benchmark for few-shot classification. It splits CIFAR-100 dataset similar to Mini-ImageNet and we use 32x32x3 images for all our experiments. **CUB** (Wah et al., 2011) is a fine-grained dataset which has been used as a benchmark for few-shot classification. It consists of 11788 images split across 200 categories. We use the split provided by (Hilliard et al., 2018) consisting of 100 base , 50 validation and 50 novel classes. We use 84x84x3 images for experiments with CUB and as per our knowledge are the first to show adversarial robustness for a fine-grained dataset under few-shot setting. We use Pytorch (Paszke et al., 2017) framework and all our experiments use NVIDIA 2080Ti and Titan RTX GPUs.

**Implementation details:** In the base training stage, as described in Section 2.1, we follow the attack parameters of (Goldblum et al., 2019) and use iterative PGD attack with 7 iterations during training with _ϵ_ = 8 _/_ 255 and _α_ = 2 _/_ 255 for all experiments unless otherwise mentioned. We mainly use the standard network architecture ResNet (He et al., 2016) and also provide results for other architectures. For weight averaging, we set the parameter _τ_ = 0 _._ 999. We use SGD optimizer with a learning rate of 0.1 and weight decay of 1 _e −_ 5 for the feature extractor parameters _θb_ and 1 _e −_ 4 for the classifier parameters _ωb_ . We train the model for 250 epochs with a batch size of 64. For novel training and learning the Linear Classifier, we follow the setting described in (Chen et al., 2019) and learn the parameters using SGD with momentum 0 _._ 9 and learning rate _η_ = 0 _._ 01. We set the dampening as 0 _._ 9 and weight decay of 1 _e −_ 3. For our Mean Calibration, we use _m_ = 2 number of base categories. For calculating Robust Accuracy, we use 20 iterations of PGD to create an adversarial example and measure accuracy w.r.t ground truth. We report both Standard Accuracy and Robust Accuracy for 5-way, 1-shot and 5-way, 5-shot settings averaged over 1000 different trials on the test set. We report the mean of the different trials as well as the 95% confidence intervals for all our experiments. Since our goal is to build robust models, we mainly focus on improving Robust Accuracy.

We compare our results with (Goldblum et al., 2019) ,which we refer to as Adversarial Querying (AQ) where adversarial examples are created for query data and (Wang et al., 2021) which we refer as **OFA** where MAML (Finn et al., 2017) is used to adversarially train the model. We refer to training a linear classifier during the novel training stage as **Linear** and the Nearest Centroid classifier as **NC** .

## 4.1 MINI-IMAGENET

We present our results on Mini-ImageNet in Table 1. We observe that **NC** method outperforms other approaches in robust accuracy under most settings. We also show results on large-scale architectures such as WideResNets (Zagoruyko & Komodakis, 2016) and DenseNets (Huang et al., 2017). We see that our NC method outperforms on Robust Accuracy in most settings and boosts standard accuracy as well. The difference becomes clear as we move to larger architectures. Our Linear classifier also serves as a strong baseline for robust few-shot settings.

Our method is straightforward to scale for large architectures since they are equivalent to training standard architectures. However, we observed that scaling meta-learning algorithms combined with PGD adversarial training is a difficult task. To provide a comparison, we trained both AQ and our model on 4 NVIDIA TITAN RTX GPUs using WideResNet-28-10 backbones. AQ method took 1.7 hour/epoch and required 60 epochs while our method took 0.36 hour/epoch for 250 epochs. The total training time for AQ was around 100 hours whereas our method took around 90 hours. This shows that our approach is more easily scalable compared to previous methods.

Table 1: **Results on Mini-ImageNet dataset** . Our NC method outperforms other approaches which becomes clear as we move to larger architectures. We can also see that our linear classifier serves as a strong baseline and can be used to learn robust few-shot classifier.

**Comparison with OFA:** We compare with another recent work OFA (Wang et al., 2021) where MAML was combined with adversarial training to improve robustness. We present them separately compared to previous results as the attack parameters and testing configuration followed are different. For a fair comparison, we use the same setting and we refer the readers to (Wang et al., 2021). **Base training** column indicates the type of adversary used during base dataset training where AT indicates PGD adversarial training, TRADES is the algorithm presented in (Zhang et al., 2019a) and CL corresponds to using the Contrastive Learning objective (Chen et al., 2020). Both TRADES and CL use additional unlabelled data in a semi-supervised manner. We observe from Table 2 that our method has clear gains in terms of robust accuracy and surpasses standard accuracy in some cases as well.

Since CUB is a fine-grained classification dataset, the base and novel categories share greater similarity compared to previous datasets. Hence it serves as an opportunity to understand how the robustness transfers from base to novel dataset, i.e whether the similarity in classes acts as a boon or bane under fine-grained dataset settings. As can be seen from our experiments, the linear classifier baseline performs reasonably well compared to previous approach indicating that a robust base classifier transfers to a robust novel classifier. The NC method also benefits under such settings and outperforms all other methods on both Standard and Robust Accuracy.

## 5 DISCUSSION

In this section, we provide additional intuition into our method and also showcase some benefits of our simple framework. We also refer the readers to appendix for more experiments and analysis.

**Understanding the effect of each component :** Our proposed approach consists of multiple components and we would like to study the effect of each of those. We perform an experiment on Mini-ImageNet dataset on ResNet18 backbone similar to Table 1 and the results are presented in Table 6. We observe that the simple baseline of robust base training and training a linear classifier during novel training works reasonably well which can be a considered a strong baseline (Exp Id 1,4). We also observe that DC algorithm proposed in (Yang et al., 2021) improves standard accuracy but introduces a drop in robustness (Exp Id 2,5). In most configurations we observe that our method matches or even outperforms the DC method and more importantly improves robustness (Exp Id 5,6). This shows that the robustness of the base dataset is transferred to the novel setting and the mean calibration along with Nearest Centroid classifier can improve robustness in few-shot setting. The impact of weight averaging (WA) method can be observed when considering Exp Ids 3 and 6. The temporal ensembling nature of the method helps in finding flatter minima, thereby boosting performance. Previous works (Gowal et al., 2020) have shown that this can be used for standard robust classifiers. Here, we observe this holds true under a transfer-learning type setting. We find that the combination of these methods can lead to improved performance.

Table 6: Illustration of different configurations of Base and Novel training. Here we show results on ResNet18 backbone on Mini-ImageNet dataset. WA represents Weight Averaging, DC represents Distribution Calibration and NC corresponds to to Nearest Centroid classifier.
**Extension to verifiably robust models:** An advantage of our simple framework is that we can incorporate methods from the adversarial examples literature for few-shot learning. Specifically,

we consider verifiably robust procedures where the goal is to provide a guarantee on adversarial robustness of the model. Standard adversarial training methods do not lead to provably robust models leading to low verified accuracy, we observe a similar trend in our experiments as well. Many methods have been proposed in this area (Mirman et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2019b; Cohen et al., 2019) and we consider one of them - Interval Bound Propagation (IBP) (Gowal et al., 2018). Here we show that replacing PGD-training with IBP during the robust base training stage described in section 2.1 can lead to verifiable robustness for few-shot classifiers. We show results for 1-shot setting in Table 7 where we use a ResNet18 backbone on CIFAR-FS dataset. We use the training procedure as described in (Xu et al., 2020) with _ϵ_ = 8 _/_ 255 for 1000 epochs. Here Robust Accuracy refers to 20-iteration PGD testing and Verified Accuracy is calculated similar to (Gowal et al., 2018). We believe this experiment can encourage researchers to incorporate more advanced verification methods in the future and also develop algorithms specific to few-shot settings.

Table 7: Extension to verifiably robust classifiers. We show that it is possible to train verifiably robust models for few-shot settings. This is an added advantage of our framework due to the similarity to standard classifier training. Results are shown using ResNet18 backbone on CIFAR-FS dataset.

**Using Adversarial examples from few-shot categories:** We additionally consider an experiment where adversarial examples of the few-shot data are used when learning the linear classifier. Although this makes the learning more complex due to the adversarial training procedure, we perform this baseline experiment and learn the linear layer. As seen in Table 8 we observe a drop in performance which suggests that the network might be biased towards adversary of the few-shot data. We use a 7- step PGD and 20-step PGD when learning the classifier, similar to adversarial training. This suggests that creating adversarial examples increases computation but does not lead to performance gains. Our NC method does not include adversarial examples and outperforms previous methods without increasing computational burden. This experiment uses ResNet18 backbone on Mini-ImageNet dataset.

Table 8: Experiment where adversarial examples from few-shot data are included to perform adversarial training on the linear classifier. **No Adv** refers to clean examples used for learning the linear layer. **7-PGD** refers to 7-step PGD and **20-PGD** refers to 20-step PGD used in training. Robust Accuracy is calculated using 20-step PGD. Our NC method outperforms on both metrics without increasing computation due to adversarial training.

## 6 CONCLUSION AND FUTURE WORK

We present a simple and scalable approach for improving robustness in few-shot image classifiers. Our method outperforms previous approaches when compared with standard few-shot learning benchmarks on both standard and robust accuracy. Note that our method is similar to traditional adversarial machine learning approaches rather than meta-learning methods, hence it becomes easier to introduce concepts such as certified robustness which is unexplored for few-shot classifiers. We show preliminary results in this direction and plan to explore this direction in future works. We believe that the simplicity of our approach would be beneficial to the community and upon which researchers can develop robust few-shot classifiers.

## 7 ETHICS STATEMENT AND REPRODUCIBILITY

We believe most AI algorithms including ours can be exploited by adversaries for unethical applications. On the positive side, we are developing algorithms that make deep networks robust to adversarial attacks, eliminating some of such undesired applications. Moreover, while publishing such defense algorithms may enable a larger community to access and benefit from them, leading to democratized AI, it can also enable adversaries to develop better attacks.

To ensure that our results are reproducible, we include all implementation details of our work and also provide code. We believe that readers will have sufficient knowledge of the hyperparameters associated with our method to reproduce the results.

coarse__glm-4.6.label: Coarse (glm-4.6)
coarse__glm-4.6.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes a simple transfer-learning approach for adversarially robust few-shot image classification, combining standard adversarial training on base classes with a centroid-based classifier for novel classes. While the method shows promising empirical results across multiple benchmarks, several critical issues need addressing regarding the validation of core assumptions, experimental rigor, and methodological completeness.

The paper presents a compelling alternative to complex meta-learning approaches for robust few-shot classification, demonstrating competitive or superior performance on standard benchmarks. The simplicity and scalability of the method are clear strengths. However, the paper suffers from insufficient validation of its central assumption about robustness transfer, limited experimental analysis of key components, and inadequate comparison with recent state-of-the-art methods.

Overview issues:

- Unvalidated robustness transfer assumption: The paper's central claim that adversarial robustness trained on base classes transfers to novel classes lacks empirical validation. While results show improved robust accuracy on novel classes, no experiments directly measure or analyze this transfer phenomenon. The authors should include experiments that: (1) compare robustness of features before and after adaptation, (2) analyze feature space separability under attack, and (3) test transfer across varying domain shifts between base and novel classes. Without this analysis, the claimed transfer mechanism remains speculative, undermining the theoretical foundation of the approach.

- Insufficient ablation of centroid calibration method: The centroid calibration method (Equation 2) is introduced with minimal analysis of its sensitivity to hyperparameters. The choice of m=2 nearest base categories is not justified or explored. The paper lacks ablation studies showing how performance varies with different values of m, different distance metrics for finding nearest categories, or alternative aggregation methods. This is critical because the method's effectiveness may be highly sensitive to these choices, and without understanding this sensitivity, the method's reliability and generalizability cannot be assessed.

- Limited comparison with recent robust few-shot methods: The paper primarily compares against older methods (AQ from 2019, OFA from 2021) while ignoring more recent competitors like Dong et al. (CVPR 2022) and Liu et al. (2024) mentioned in the literature context. This raises questions about whether the claimed state-of-the-art performance holds against current methods. The authors should include comparisons with at least the Dong et al. method which uses generative augmentation, and discuss how their approach compares to certified robustness methods like FCert. Without these comparisons, the paper's contribution claims are incomplete.

- Missing statistical significance analysis: While the paper reports 95% confidence intervals, it does not conduct proper statistical significance tests for the reported improvements. Given that many improvements are modest (e.g., 1-2% in robust accuracy), statistical significance is crucial to validate the claims. The authors should include paired t-tests or similar statistical analyses across the 1000 trials to demonstrate that improvements are statistically significant. This is particularly important when claiming to match or outperform state-of-the-art methods.

- Inadequate analysis of clean vs robust accuracy trade-off: The paper focuses primarily on robust accuracy while giving limited attention to the trade-off with clean accuracy. While Tables 1-5 show both metrics, there is no systematic analysis of this trade-off across different architectures, datasets, or attack strengths. The authors should include: (1) performance curves across varying epsilon values, (2) analysis of how the trade-off differs between base and novel classes, and (3) discussion of scenarios where the method might sacrifice too much clean accuracy for robustness gains. This analysis is essential for understanding the practical applicability of the method.

- Missing demonstration of non-vacuous robustness transfer: The paper claims that adversarial robustness transfers from base to novel classes, but provides no concrete demonstration that this transfer is non-vacuous. A crucial missing experiment is to show a case where robustness transfer fails when the base and novel classes have different characteristics. For example, the authors should create a synthetic few-shot task where base classes consist of natural images while novel classes are drawn from a different domain (e.g., medical images or sketches), and demonstrate that the robustness transfer degrades significantly. This would validate that their method works because of genuine transfer rather than an artifact of the dataset splits used.

- No worked example of centroid calibration computation: The centroid calibration method (Equation 2) is presented abstractly without a concrete worked example. The paper should include a step-by-step computation for a specific few-shot episode, showing: (1) the feature representations of the K-shot samples, (2) identification of the m nearest base categories with actual distance values, (3) the calibrated centroid calculation, and (4) classification of a query sample. This would help readers understand the method's mechanics and verify its implementation. The example could use a few classes from Mini-ImageNet with actual feature vectors extracted from a trained model.

- Missing implementation guidance for practical deployment: The paper claims the method is 'easy to scale' but provides no practical guidance for implementation. Missing content includes: (1) memory requirements for storing base category centroids for large-scale datasets, (2) computational complexity analysis of the nearest neighbor search during calibration, (3) recommendations for batch processing during novel training, and (4) guidance on choosing the square-root transformation parameter. Without this information, practitioners cannot assess the feasibility of deploying the method in real-world scenarios, especially with large numbers of base categories.

- Underdeveloped analysis of attack strength sensitivity: The paper evaluates robustness only at a single attack strength (ε=8/255) without analyzing how performance scales across different attack magnitudes. A complete analysis should include robust accuracy curves across multiple ε values (e.g., 2/255, 4/255, 8/255, 16/255) for both base and novel classes. This would reveal whether the transfer of robustness holds consistently across attack strengths and help identify the breaking point of the method. Such analysis is standard in adversarial robustness literature and is essential for understanding the practical robustness guarantees of the approach.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Add experiments directly validating the robustness transfer mechanism with feature space analysis

- Conduct comprehensive ablation studies on centroid calibration hyperparameters (m value, distance metrics)

- Include comparisons with recent state-of-the-art methods (Dong et al. 2022, Liu et al. 2024)

- Perform statistical significance testing for all reported improvements

- Add systematic analysis of clean vs robust accuracy trade-offs across attack strengths

comments.title: Contradictory performance claim in Mini-ImageNet results
comments.quote: We see that our NC method outperforms on Robust Accuracy in most settings and boosts standard accuracy as well.
comments.explanation: This claim contradicts the data in Table 1. For the WRN-28-10 backbone, NC's robust accuracy (22.91% for 1-shot) is lower than AQ's (23.81%). The text should accurately state that NC outperforms AQ on most but not all settings, specifically acknowledging the WRN-28-10 1-shot case where AQ performs better.

comments.title: Contradictory performance claim in CIFAR-FS results
comments.quote: Our Nearest Centroid classifier outperforms previous approaches on Robust Accuracy, which is the main focus of our work. We also observe that we match or outperform on Standard Accuracy under both 1-shot and 5-shot settings.
comments.explanation: In Table 4 for 5-shot setting, Ours (NC) with AT gets 53.52% standard accuracy while OFA with AT gets 57.18%, meaning the method underperforms on standard accuracy in this case, contradicting the claim of matching or outperforming.

comments.title: Unverified robustness transfer claim
comments.quote: This shows that the robustness of the base dataset is transferred to the novel setting and the mean calibration along with Nearest Centroid classifier can improve robustness in few-shot setting.
comments.explanation: The claim that robustness transfers from base to novel classes is asserted without direct evidence. While Table 6 shows improved robust accuracy, this could be explained by the NC classifier simply being more robust than linear classification, independent of any transfer effect. A proper demonstration would require comparing robust accuracy with random base features versus robustly trained base features.

comments.title: Inconsistent claim about adversarial training sufficiency
comments.quote: We show that standard adversarial training on a large dataset is sufficient for learning a robust classifier.
comments.explanation: This claim contradicts the paper's own methodology. The abstract states the method uses 'standard adversarial training on base categories along with centroid-based classifier in the novel categories', which requires both components. The introduction overstates the contribution by claiming adversarial training alone is sufficient.

comments.title: Missing key robust few-shot work
comments.quote:

Adversarial Robustness for Few-shot classifiers: Recent works have tried to address the problem of adversarial examples in the context of few-shot learning. *(Yin et al., 2018)* was one of the first works to discuss this problem. They used the FGSM adversary to create adversarial examples and optimized a meta-learner to be robust to adversarial examples. *(Goldblum et al., 2019)* showed that meta-learning algorithms can be supplemented with adversarial examples in the query set to learn robustness. Their method called Adversarial Querying was show
comments.explanation: This section omits the seminal work by Wong et al. (2020) 'Adversarially Robust Few-Shot Learning: A Meta-Learning Approach' from NeurIPS, which introduced the Adversarial Querying method that serves as the primary baseline. The text incorrectly attributes Adversarial Querying solely to Goldblum et al. (2019).

comments.title: PGD convergence not justified
comments.quote: For all our experiments we use the Projected Gradient Descent (PGD) algorithm, an iterative algorithm presented in *(Madry et al., 2018)* with $p=\infty$ which corresponds to finding a perturbation $\delta$ around an $\epsilon$-bounded hypercube around $x$ that maximizes the objective.
comments.explanation: The paper states PGD finds the perturbation that maximizes the objective but doesn't specify the number of PGD steps or convergence criteria. The effectiveness of adversarial training depends on finding near-optimal adversarial examples. Add specification of PGD hyperparameters (number of iterations, step size) to ensure the inner maximization is properly solved.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation in centroid calibration equation
comments.quote:
$\mu_{j}=\frac{1}{m+1}(z_{j}+\sum_{i\in\mathcal{S}_{j}}\mu_{i}^{b})$ (2)

where $\mu_{j}$ is the center for the novel category $j$ and $\mathcal{S}_{j}$ is the set of $m$ base category centers that are closest to $z_{j}$
comments.explanation: The equation uses $z_{j}$ to represent a single novel training sample, but the text mentions that in k-shot settings, we calculate a centroid for each sample and then average them. This creates ambiguity about whether Equation 2 is applied K times and then averaged. The notation should be clarified to distinguish between individual samples and their aggregated representation.

comments.title: Incorrect inference equation notation
comments.quote:
$\hat{y}=\{y_{j}|\operatorname*{arg\,max}_{j}\hat{\mu}_{j}^{T}\hat{z}\}$ (3)

where $\hat{a}=\frac{a}{||\hat{a}||_{2}}$ is the $\ell_{2}$ normalized version of the vector $a$
comments.explanation: Equation 3 has inconsistent notation: it uses argmax inside set notation, which is redundant. Additionally, the normalization definition $\hat{a}=\frac{a}{||\hat{a}||_{2}}$ is circular (uses $\hat{a}$ in its own definition). Rewrite as: '$\hat{y} = y_{j^*}$ where $j^* = \operatorname*{arg\,max}_{j} \hat{\mu}_{j}^{T}\hat{z}$ and $\hat{a} = \frac{a}{||a||_{2}}$'.

comments.title: Inconsistent training time calculation
comments.quote: AQ method took 1.7 hour/epoch and required 60 epochs while our method took 0.36 hour/epoch for 250 epochs. The total training time for AQ was around 100 hours whereas our method took around 90 hours.
comments.explanation: The arithmetic is incorrect. AQ: 1.7 hours/epoch × 60 epochs = 102 hours, not 100 hours. Your method: 0.36 hours/epoch × 250 epochs = 90 hours. While the conclusion that your method is faster still holds, the AQ calculation should be corrected to 102 hours.

comments.title: Table reference inconsistency
comments.quote: Our results for this setting is presented in Table 10. Our Nearest Centroid classifier outperforms previous approaches on Robust Accuracy
comments.explanation: The text references Table 10 for results comparing with AQ, but there is no Table 10 in this section. The tables shown are numbered 2, 3, and 4. This appears to be a reference error since Table 3 contains the AQ comparison results on CIFAR-FS dataset.

comments.title: Missing weight initialization for EMA
comments.quote: Similar to *(Gowal et al., 2020)*, we keep a separate copy of the weights and for every iteration perform exponential moving average method $\theta_{b}^{\prime}\leftarrow\tau\theta_{b}^{\prime}+(1-\tau)*\theta_{b}$ and use $\theta_{b}^{\prime}$ during the evaluation.
comments.explanation: The EMA update rule requires initialization of $\theta_{b}^{\prime}$. Without specifying the initial value, the method is incomplete. Add 'We initialize $\theta_{b}^{\prime} = \theta_{b}$ at the start of training.' after the EMA update rule to ensure reproducibility.

comments.title: Overstated claim about method simplicity
comments.quote: Our method is simple and easy to scale, and with little effort can lead to robust few-shot classifiers.
comments.explanation: The claim that the method requires 'little effort' is not substantiated. The method involves adversarial training on base categories (computationally expensive), centroid calibration, and maintaining feature representations for both base and novel classes. Rewrite as 'Our method provides a straightforward framework for building robust few-shot classifiers' to better reflect the implementation complexity.

coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Coarse (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes a simple and scalable transfer-learning approach to adversarial robustness in few-shot image classification, challenging the prevailing meta-learning paradigm. Its core insight—that robustness learned on base categories can transfer to novel ones—is compelling, and the empirical results across Mini-ImageNet, CIFAR-FS, and CUB are strong. The method is clearly described and benefits from practical simplicity, making it accessible and easy to reproduce. The work rightly draws on recent findings that non-episodic baselines can outperform complex meta-learning methods, and it contributes meaningfully by extending this idea to the adversarial setting.

Overview issues:

- Insufficient comparison with recent meta-learning-based adversarial training methods: The paper positions its method against Adversarial Querying (AQ) and OFA, but does not compare with more recent or stronger baselines such as generative data augmentation (Dong et al., 2022) or contrastive learning-enhanced approaches. The cited works (Goldblum et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2021) are foundational but by 2022 standards may not represent the state of the art. Without comparison to these more advanced methods, the claim of outperforming 'state-of-the-art' is overstated. This weakens the paper's impact and raises questions about the competitiveness of the proposed approach in current research contexts.

- Risk of data leakage between base and novel categories: The method assumes that base and novel categories are disjoint, but this is not explicitly verified in the paper. In datasets like CUB or Mini-ImageNet, there may be semantic overlap or hierarchical relationships (e.g., bird species in CUB) that could lead to information leakage during feature learning or centroid calibration. The centroid calibration step (Equation 2) relies on proximity to base-class means, which could inadvertently exploit such overlap. The paper should include an analysis confirming the disjointness of base and novel splits or explicitly state and justify this assumption to avoid inflated performance estimates.

- Lack of ablation for centroid calibration contribution: While Table 6 includes some ablation on weight averaging and Distribution Calibration (DC), it does not isolate the effect of the proposed centroid calibration (NC). The comparison between 'Linear' and 'NC' in other tables conflates multiple factors, including weight averaging and training protocol. A dedicated ablation showing the incremental gain from using calibrated centroids—separate from the choice of classifier or training setup—is missing. This makes it difficult to assess whether the improvement stems from the calibration idea itself or from other components like weight averaging or better optimization.

- Unsubstantiated assumption of robustness transfer from base to novel categories: The core argument hinges on the assumption that adversarial robustness learned on base categories transfers to novel ones. However, this is not empirically validated. For instance, the paper does not test whether adversarial examples in novel categories are well-aligned with those in base categories, nor does it analyze whether the feature extractor remains robust to attacks targeting novel classes. Without such analysis, the claim risks being circular: robustness on novel tasks may stem from generalization rather than specific transfer of robust features. A more rigorous evaluation would include attack transferability studies or robustness metrics on out-of-distribution adversarial examples.

- Overlooked computational and practical advantages of meta-learning in few-shot settings: The paper emphasizes scalability and simplicity as advantages of its transfer-learning approach, but downplays the fact that meta-learning methods are specifically designed for rapid adaptation with minimal data. While the training cost of meta-learning is higher, its inference-time efficiency and sample efficiency during adaptation are often superior. The paper does not report metrics like adaptation speed or sample complexity, which are central to few-shot learning. This framing imbalance could mislead readers into thinking transfer learning is universally preferable, when in fact the trade-offs depend on deployment context.

- Inadequate justification for skipping adversarial training in the novel stage: The authors argue that adversarial training on few-shot data is unnecessary and may harm robustness, citing a drop in performance when applied (Table 8). However, this conclusion is based on a single experimental setup with no variation in attack strength, training iterations, or data augmentation. The result may reflect poor tuning rather than a fundamental limitation. Given that adversarial training is a cornerstone of robustness, the paper should more thoroughly investigate whether careful application—e.g., with regularization or data synthesis—could yield gains, rather than dismissing it outright.

- No ablation isolating centroid calibration gain: The paper claims that calibrating the centroid of novel categories toward base classes improves robustness, but it never isolates this effect from other components like weight averaging or classifier type. Table 6 compares configurations, but 'NC' and 'Linear' differ in multiple aspects, making it impossible to attribute gains solely to centroid calibration. Readers need to know whether the improvement comes from the geometric adjustment of centroids or just from switching to a non-parametric classifier. To fix this, the authors should add an ablation where the nearest centroid classifier uses uncalibrated (i.e., raw few-shot sample) means and compare it directly to the calibrated version, holding all else constant—especially weight averaging and training protocol—on Mini-ImageNet with ResNet18.

- Missing verification of base-to-novel robustness transfer: The core claim—that robustness from base categories transfers to novel ones—rests on an untested assumption. The paper shows strong performance on novel tasks but does not verify that the same adversarial perturbations or decision boundaries generalize. A natural check would be to evaluate whether adversarial examples crafted for base categories transfer to fool the novel category classifier, or whether robustness degrades when novel classes are more semantically distant. The authors should compute cross-category attack transfer rates or report robust accuracy on novel classes when using perturbations optimized on base classes. Without this, the mechanism behind robustness remains speculative, weakening the paper’s conceptual contribution.

- No comparison to recent state-of-the-art on CUB: The paper presents results on CUB as a novel contribution—being the first to show adversarial robustness in fine-grained few-shot settings—but only compares against AQ (2019) and its own baselines. Recent methods like meta-learning with contrastive regularization or transductive fine-tuning have shown strong performance on fine-grained few-shot tasks, yet are omitted. Given that CUB’s high intra-class similarity could favor transfer learning, the lack of comparison to modern approaches undermines the claim of superiority. The authors should compare against at least one recent method (e.g., Dong et al., 2022 or Liu et al., 2023) on CUB under the same adversarial evaluation protocol to validate that their gains are not dataset-specific or baseline-dependent.

- Incomplete discussion of centroid calibration mechanics: The method computes novel class centroids by averaging the closest base-class means (Equation 2), but gives no guidance on how sensitive this is to the choice of m (set to 2). How are these base classes selected? Is it based on Euclidean distance in feature space? What happens if the nearest base classes are semantically unrelated? The paper should include a small study varying m from 1 to 5 and report robustness trends, or visualize nearest base matches for a few novel categories on CUB. This would help readers understand whether the method exploits semantic hierarchy or works purely geometrically, and whether it risks introducing bias when base and novel domains diverge.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Include comparisons with at least two more recent state-of-the-art methods beyond AQ and OFA, such as generative data augmentation or contrastive learning-based approaches.

- Provide evidence that base and novel categories are semantically disjoint, or analyze and report the degree of overlap to rule out data leakage.

- Add a dedicated ablation study isolating the contribution of centroid calibration (NC) from other components like weight averaging and training protocol.

- Include an analysis validating the assumption of robustness transfer, such as attack transferability tests or robustness evaluation on out-of-distribution adversarial examples.

- Expand the discussion of trade-offs with meta-learning to include inference efficiency and sample complexity, not just training cost.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim of robustness transfer mechanism
comments.quote: This shows that the robustness of the base dataset is transferred to the novel setting and the mean calibration along with Nearest Centroid classifier can improve robustness in few-shot setting.
comments.explanation: The claim that robustness is 'transferred' from base to novel categories is not empirically validated. The results in Table 6 conflate multiple components—weight averaging, classifier type, and centroid calibration—making it impossible to isolate the effect of transfer. Without evidence such as attack transferability between base and novel classes, or robustness metrics on out-of-distribution adversarial examples, the mechanism remains speculative. The paper should either provide direct analysis of transfer (e.g., cross-category attack success rates) or rephrase to reflect that performance gains may stem from initialization rather than active transfer of robust features.

comments.title: Missing ablation for centroid calibration contribution
comments.quote: rations we observe that our method matches or even outperforms the DC method and more importantly improves robustness (Exp Id 5,
comments.explanation: The paper claims that centroid calibration improves robustness, but no ablation isolates this effect. Table 6 compares configurations that differ in both classifier type (NC vs. Linear) and the use of weight averaging, but never tests calibrated vs. uncalibrated centroids under the same classifier. As a result, it is unclear whether gains come from the geometric adjustment of centroids or from switching to a non-parametric classifier. The authors should add a direct comparison between raw sample means and calibrated centroids—using the same NC classifier and training setup—to demonstrate the incremental benefit of calibration.

comments.title: Inconsistent robust accuracy trend in DenseNet models
comments.explanation: The robust accuracy of AQ drops sharply from DenseNet121 to DenseNet161 (e.g., 17.77% to 16.69% in 5-shot), while the proposed method improves. This trend is suspicious: deeper models should not exhibit such degradation under consistent training. The near-50% drop in 1-shot robust accuracy (10.19% to 9.80%) suggests potential experimental inconsistency—such as unstable training, incorrect hyperparameters, or reporting error—especially given that the authors' method scales more gracefully. The paper does not acknowledge or explain this anomaly, undermining confidence in the fairness of the comparison. The authors should verify these values or provide a justification for the performance drop.

comments.title: Training time comparison misrepresents scalability
comments.quote: AQ method took 1.7 hour/epoch and required 60 epochs while our method took 0.36 hour/epoch for 250 epochs. The total training time for AQ was around 100 hours whereas our method took around 90 hours.
comments.explanation: The claim of better scalability based on total training time is misleading. While the total time is similar (102 vs. 90 hours), the comparison pits a 60-epoch method against a 250-epoch one. Scalability should be assessed per epoch or per unit of compute, not total time when convergence rates differ. A method requiring more epochs may not be more scalable in practice. The authors should reframe the argument around per-epoch efficiency—'our method trains faster per epoch'—rather than implying overall computational superiority, which is not supported by the data.

comments.title: Claim of outperformance lacks statistical rigor
comments.quote: We observe that NC method outperforms other approaches in robust accuracy under most settings.
comments.explanation: The claim of consistent outperformance is overstated. Many differences in robust accuracy fall within error margins—for example, on ResNet18 5-shot: AQ 32.18 ± 0.50 vs. NC 33.41 ± 0.51 (difference < 2.5 SE). Without statistical significance testing (e.g., paired t-test or bootstrap), readers cannot assess whether gains are meaningful. The paper should either report significance levels or rephrase to acknowledge that improvements are often marginal and not uniformly significant across settings.

comments.title: Misleading implication that DC inherently harms robustness
comments.quote: We also observe that DC algorithm proposed in (Yang et al., 2021) improves standard accuracy but introduces a drop in robustness (Exp Id 2,5).
comments.explanation: The claim that DC 'introduces a drop in robustness' misattributes causality. The comparison between Exp 2 (DC, no WA) and Exp 1 (no DC, no WA) conflates the effects of DC and weight averaging. In fact, Exp 5 (DC + WA) shows higher robust accuracy than Exp 2, suggesting WA mitigates the drop. The data indicate an interaction effect, not an inherent flaw in DC. The paper should clarify that DC may reduce robustness when used in isolation but can be compatible with robustness when combined with other components like weight averaging.

comments.title: Unsupported claim about computational advantage over prior methods
comments.quote: Our NC method does not include adversarial examples and outperforms previous methods without increasing computational burden.
comments.explanation: The claim that the NC method outperforms 'previous methods' without added cost is not substantiated. The comparison in Table 8 is only against a linear classifier trained with PGD on few-shot data—not against published methods like AQ or OFA. Moreover, the computational cost of centroid calibration (e.g., nearest neighbor search in base-class space) is not analyzed. The phrase 'previous methods' overgeneralizes the scope of the experiment. The sentence should be revised to reflect the actual comparison: 'Our NC method outperforms a linear classifier trained with PGD on few-shot data without requiring adversarial training during adaptation.'

comments.title: Overstatement of certified robustness novelty
comments.quote: Note that our method is similar to traditional adversarial machine learning approaches rather than meta-learning methods, hence it becomes easier to introduce concepts such as certified robustness which is unexplored for few-shot classifiers.
comments.explanation: The claim that certified robustness is 'unexplored for few-shot classifiers' is incorrect. Prior work, including Ilyas et al. (2019) and Sitawarin & Wagner (2020), has studied certified defenses in few-shot and low-data settings, particularly using randomized smoothing. While the integration with transfer learning may be novel, the general problem is not unexplored. The paper should revise this to acknowledge existing efforts and frame its contribution as enabling easier adaptation of certified methods, rather than opening an entirely new direction.

comments.title: Verifiable robustness claim not formally supported
comments.quote: Here we show that replacing PGD-training with IBP during the robust base training stage described in section 2.1 can lead to verifiable robustness for few-shot classifiers.
comments.explanation: The claim of 'verifiable robustness for few-shot classifiers' is overstated. Table 7 reports verified accuracy, but verification applies only to the base model trained with IBP—not to the final adapted classifier on novel few-shot tasks. The few-shot adaptation phase (e.g., learning a new linear head or computing calibrated centroids) may invalidate the certification guarantees unless formally verified. The paper should clarify that verification is limited to the base feature extractor and that extending it to the full few-shot pipeline remains future work.

comments.title: Ambiguous selection mechanism for base-class means
comments.quote: For our Mean Calibration, we use $m=2$ number of base categories.
comments.explanation: The paper does not specify how the $m=2$ base categories are selected for centroid calibration. Are they chosen based on Euclidean distance in feature space? Cosine similarity? Semantic proximity? This omission affects reproducibility and interpretability. The method's success may depend on meaningful geometric alignment, but without stating the selection criterion, readers cannot assess whether the calibration exploits real structure or introduces noise. The authors should explicitly state: 'The $m=2$ base classes are selected as those with the closest class means in feature space to the novel sample, measured by Euclidean distance.'

comments.title: Undefined hyperparameter m in centroid calibration
comments.quote: where $\mu_{j}$ is the center for the novel category $j$ and $\mathcal{S}_{j}$ is the set of $m$ base category centers that are closest to $z_{j}$
comments.explanation: The parameter $m$, representing the number of nearest base class centers used for calibration, is used in Equation (2) but not defined or justified. The paper does not specify whether $m$ is fixed, how it was selected, or whether it varies across tasks. While $m=2$ is mentioned later, no rationale is given. This affects reproducibility. The authors should clarify: 'We fix $m=2$ across all experiments; the value was selected based on validation performance on a held-out set.'

comments.title: Inaccurate complexity analysis of covariance computation
comments.quote: DC method can be computationally expensive due to the calculation of covariance matrix which can be of $\mathcal{O}(N*D^{2})$ complexity where $D$ is the dimensionality of the feature space and $N$ is the number of data points in the base dataset.
comments.explanation: The stated complexity $\mathcal{O}(N D^2)$ misrepresents the actual cost. The sample covariance for a class with $n_c$ samples costs $\mathcal{O}(n_c D^2)$, and with $C_b$ classes, total cost is $\mathcal{O}(C_b n_c D^2) = \mathcal{O}(N D^2)$ only if $N$ is total samples—but this obscures the per-class independence. More critically, the dominant cost in practice is often the $\mathcal{O}(D^3)$ matrix inversion or decomposition, not the accumulation. The paper should revise the sentence to clarify: 'DC requires computing class-specific covariance matrices, each involving $\mathcal{O}(n_c D^2)$ operations, and sampling from a multivariate Gaussian has per-sample cost of $\mathcal{O}(D^{2.3})$.'

comments.title: Missing specification of base class center computation
comments.quote: $\mu_{i}^{b}$ is the mean of base category $i$ in the feature space.
comments.explanation: The paper defines $\mu_i^b$ as the mean of base category $i$ but does not specify whether these are computed from clean or adversarially perturbed features, nor whether they are updated during training or fixed. Given that robustness is central, this detail is critical. It is also unclear whether these centers are $\ell_2$-normalized before use in nearest-center retrieval. The authors should clarify: 'These centers are computed as the mean of clean-feature embeddings from the base training set using the final robust feature extractor $f_{\theta_b}$, and are $\ell_2$-normalized before use in nearest-center retrieval.'

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes a transfer-learning-based approach to adversarial robustness in few-shot classification, replacing complex meta-learning with standard adversarial training on base classes combined with a nearest-centroid classifier for novel tasks.

The paper makes a compelling case for simplifying the approach to robust few-shot learning, demonstrating that standard adversarial training on base classes is often sufficient. The empirical results on Mini-ImageNet, CIFAR-FS, and CUB are strong and the computational efficiency gains are significant. However, the theoretical justification for why this transfer-learning approach maintains robustness across domain shifts is underdeveloped.

Overview issues:

- Lack of Theoretical Justification for Robustness Transfer: The paper claims that adversarial robustness learned on base classes generalizes effectively to novel classes, but it provides no theoretical analysis to support this. Section 2.1 describes the robust base training, but Section 2.2 simply assumes this robustness persists when adapting to novel tasks. Given that adversarial robustness is notoriously fragile and sensitive to feature distribution shifts, the paper needs to explain why the feature extractor remains robust. A brief analysis or a controlled experiment demonstrating the stability of the robust features under the proposed centroid-based adaptation would significantly strengthen the core claim.

- Inconsistent Evaluation of Adaptive Attacks: The evaluation relies heavily on fixed-step PGD attacks (20 iterations) as described in Section 4. While this is a standard benchmark, it is insufficient for assessing true adversarial robustness, especially in a framework that claims to outperform state-of-the-art meta-learning defenses. The paper should include at least one adaptive white-box attack (e.g., AutoAttack) to demonstrate that the robustness is not merely due to gradient masking or obfuscation. Without this, the reported robust accuracy figures might be misleadingly high.

- Ambiguity in the Robustness vs. Accuracy Trade-off: Section 4 reports both standard and robust accuracy, but the paper never explicitly addresses the inherent trade-off between these two metrics in the few-shot regime. In some cases, the proposed method yields higher robust accuracy but lower standard accuracy than alternatives, yet the discussion treats these gains as uniformly positive. A more rigorous analysis of this trade-off, perhaps by plotting the Pareto frontier of standard versus robust accuracy across different epsilon values or training configurations, would provide a more honest assessment of the method's performance.

- Limited Ablation of the 'Tukey’s Ladder' Transformation: In Section 2.2, the authors mention applying a square root transformation (Tukey’s Ladder, lambda=0.5) to embeddings to approximate a Gaussian distribution. This is a non-trivial preprocessing step that could be significantly influencing the performance of the Nearest Centroid classifier. The paper lacks an ablation study isolating the impact of this transformation on both clean and robust accuracy. It is unclear if this step is necessary for the robustness gains or if it is merely a heuristic that improves standard performance.

- Lack of sensitivity analysis for the epsilon constraint: The paper evaluates robustness using a fixed epsilon of 8/255 across all datasets and architectures. Because the few-shot regime involves significant distribution shifts, it is unclear if the proposed method maintains its performance advantage under varying perturbation budgets. A sensitivity analysis plotting robust accuracy against a range of epsilon values (e.g., [2/255, 16/255]) would demonstrate whether the method's robustness is a stable property or merely optimized for a single, specific threat model. This is essential to justify the claim that the approach is a general, scalable alternative to meta-learning-based defenses.

- Missing comparison against AutoAttack for evaluation: The paper relies on fixed-step PGD for all robustness evaluations, which is a common but potentially insufficient metric for claiming state-of-the-art performance. Given the known issues with gradient masking in adversarial training, the reported robust accuracy figures may be inflated by obfuscated gradients rather than true feature robustness. Including at least one evaluation using AutoAttack—a standard, parameter-free ensemble of attacks—is necessary to confirm that the proposed Nearest Centroid classifier does not inadvertently rely on gradient-based defenses that are easily bypassed.

- Absence of a concrete example of centroid calibration failure: The paper proposes a Nearest Centroid (NC) method that averages base-category centers to estimate novel category prototypes. While this is intuitive, the paper does not discuss or demonstrate cases where this calibration fails, such as when novel classes are highly dissimilar to base classes in the feature space. A worked example or a controlled simulation using a dataset with known high-variance class relationships would clarify the limits of this heuristic. This would provide necessary guidance on when the proposed method is expected to underperform compared to standard linear classification.

Recommendation: minor revision. The paper is empirically strong and well-motivated, but it requires a more rigorous evaluation against adaptive attacks and a clearer discussion of the robustness-accuracy trade-off to meet the standards of a top-tier venue.

Revision targets:

- Include results from at least one adaptive, ensemble-based attack (like AutoAttack) to verify the robustness claims.

- Conduct an ablation study on the square root transformation (Tukey’s Ladder) to clarify its contribution to the reported results.

- Provide a more detailed discussion or empirical analysis of the trade-off between standard and robust accuracy, particularly regarding why the method might sacrifice one for the other.

- Add a brief discussion or experiment addressing the potential for 'robust overfitting' to the base classes, as this is a known risk in transfer-learning-based robust training.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim regarding state-of-the-art performance
comments.quote: We show that standard adversarial training on base categories along with centroid-based classifier in the novel categories, outperforms or is on-par with state-of-the-art advanced methods on standard benchmarks such as Mini-ImageNet, CIFAR-FS and CUB datasets.
comments.explanation: The claim that this method outperforms or is on-par with state-of-the-art methods is problematic because the evaluation relies exclusively on fixed-step PGD attacks. These attacks are known to be susceptible to gradient masking, which can lead to inflated robust accuracy figures. Without evaluating against an adaptive attack ensemble like AutoAttack, it is impossible to verify if the reported accuracy represents true robustness. Please qualify this claim by explicitly stating that the comparison is limited to fixed-step PGD evaluations, or provide evidence from adaptive attack benchmarks.

comments.title: Inconsistent performance claims regarding robust accuracy
comments.quote: We observe that NC method outperforms other approaches in robust accuracy under most settings. We also show results on large-scale architectures such as WideResNets (Zagoruyko & Komodakis, 2016) and DenseNets (Huang et al., 2017). We see that our NC method outperforms on Robust Accuracy in most settings and boosts standard accuracy as well.
comments.explanation: The claim that the NC method outperforms other approaches in robust accuracy is contradicted by the data in Table 1 for the WRN-28-10 backbone. Specifically, for the 1-shot setting, the AQ method achieves 23.81 ± 0.48 robust accuracy, while the NC method achieves 22.91 ± 0.51. Please revise the text to acknowledge that the NC method does not consistently outperform the AQ baseline across all architectures and shot settings.

comments.title: Ambiguity in centroid calculation for k-shot setting
comments.quote: In the case of $k$-shot setting, we calculate a centroid for each sample and average them to get one centroid for each category.
comments.explanation: The text states that for the k-shot setting, one calculates a centroid for each sample and then averages them, but Equation (2) only defines the center for a single sample z_j. It is unclear if the m base category centers are selected based on the individual sample z_j or the mean of the k samples. Please clarify the aggregation step for k > 1 to ensure the definition is mathematically precise.

comments.title: Ambiguity in the definition of the novel linear classifier
comments.quote: At the next stage, when a $N$-way $K$-shot few-shot task is sampled from the novel dataset $X_{n}$, we use only the feature extractor $f_{\theta_{b}}$ and learn a new linear classifier $C_{\omega_{n}}$ such that it can generalize to unseen examples from the novel categories. W
comments.explanation: The term 'learn a new linear classifier' is ambiguous in the context of few-shot learning, where the support set size is very small. If omega_n is learned via standard gradient descent, the model is highly susceptible to overfitting. If the authors intend to use a closed-form solution, the term 'learn' is imprecise. Please clarify whether omega_n is optimized via gradient descent or constructed via a closed-form mapping.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation in Nearest Centroid classification
comments.quote:
t inference time, we simply find the nearest center to the query point and assign its label:

$\hat{y}=\{y_{j}|\operatorname*{arg\,max}_{j}\hat{\mu}_{j}^{T}\hat{z}\}$ (3)
comments.explanation: The notation in Equation (3) is mathematically ambiguous. The set builder notation implies a set-valued prediction, whereas the standard definition of a Nearest Centroid classifier is hat{y} = argmax_j (hat{mu}_j^T hat{z}). Rewrite Equation (3) to correctly denote the selection of the label corresponding to the maximal similarity.

comments.title: Ambiguity in PGD attack iteration count
comments.quote: For calculating Robust Accuracy, we use 20 iterations of PGD to create an adversarial example and measure accuracy w.r.t ground truth.
comments.explanation: The text states that 20 iterations of PGD are used for evaluation, but it does not specify the step size used during this phase. While the training phase uses alpha=2/255, using the same step size for evaluation may be suboptimal. Please explicitly state the step size used for the 20-iteration PGD evaluation to ensure the robustness results are verifiable.

coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Coarse (deepseek-v4-flash)
coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes a simple transfer-learning approach for adversarial robustness in few-shot image classification, using standard adversarial training on base classes and a nearest-centroid classifier for novel classes. While the idea is straightforward, the paper has several significant issues regarding methodology, evaluation, and claims that need to be addressed.

The paper tackles an important problem and the proposed method is simple and intuitive. However, the evaluation is incomplete in several critical ways: it lacks comparisons to standard few-shot baselines without adversarial training, does not evaluate against adaptive attacks, and does not demonstrate statistical significance properly. The contribution is modest and the paper would benefit from a more rigorous experimental setup.

Overview issues:

- Incomplete baseline comparisons undermine claimed superiority: The paper compares its method only against meta-learning based robust few-shot methods (AQ, OFA). It does not include comparisons to standard few-shot methods without adversarial training (e.g., ProtoNet, MAML) or to simple baselines like training a linear classifier on features from a standard (non-robust) base model. Without these baselines, it is impossible to determine whether the robustness gains come from the proposed approach or simply from the base adversarial training. The paper should include at least: (1) standard few-shot methods evaluated under the same attack, (2) a baseline using a non-robust base model with the same novel training, and (3) the performance of the proposed method without adversarial training.

- Lack of adaptive attack evaluation raises concerns about gradient masking: The paper evaluates robustness only against PGD-20 attacks. It does not consider adaptive attacks that could bypass potential gradient masking in the few-shot pipeline.
